text: The laparoscopic view shows the gallbladder positioned under the liver. The surgeon uses a fundus holder to grasp the gallbladder's fundus and a Maryland curved dissector for tissue manipulation.

The surgeon uses a Maryland curved dissector to peel off the fat and peritoneum from the junction of the gallbladder and cystic duct. The peritoneum over the cystic artery is dissected anteriorly, revealing the anatomy of the cystic duct as it joins the common bile duct below. The surgeon continues to clear the area by removing layers of fat and peritoneum for better visualization before clipping.

The surgeon clears the fog from the laparoscopic camera and re-enters the abdominal cavity, using a blunt dissector and fundus holder to grasp the gallbladder. The posterior dissection involves peeling the gallbladder from the liver fossa to increase the surgical field. The surgeon carefully dissects anteriorly between the cystic artery and cystic duct, navigating through thickened and inflamed tissue due to chronic inflammation. The gallbladder appears slippery and surrounded by fat.

The surgeon coagulates and tears a vein to prevent bleeding, which could obscure the view. The fat and peritoneum are peeled off from the cystic artery, which is clearly visible as it rides onto the gallbladder. Another vessel, possibly a cystic vein, is coagulated. The cystic artery and duct are identified, with the junction between the cystic duct and the horizontally lying common bile duct clearly visible.

The surgeon uses a clip applicator to apply clips onto the cystic artery and cystic duct. The Maryland dissector is inserted through the port to assist in positioning the clips securely. The clips are applied at the base of the cystic artery and on the cystic duct, ensuring they are pressed firmly to prevent loosening. The gallbladder is relaxed to avoid clipping the common bile duct, maintaining its free flow of bile.

The surgeon applies clips to the cystic duct and cystic artery, preparing to cut these structures. The posterior and anterior branches of the cystic artery are clearly dissected to prevent bleeding. Using monopolar cautery, the surgeon carefully detaches the gallbladder from the liver bed, pushing it upwards while navigating the thickened fascia and peritoneum.

The surgeon coagulates small capillaries and blood vessels in the gallbladder fossa using cauterization, ensuring hemostasis after the gallbladder has been successfully separated from the liver bed. Suction is applied to clear the surgical field.

The surgeon checks the cystic duct and cystic artery to ensure clips are properly applied, while suction is used to remove any residual blood from the gallbladder fossa. A suction drain is inserted to manage serum and blood ooze, ensuring no bleeding or bile leakage is present.
